message:
fix(atc-operator): silence rollup-refresh WARN spam; WARN only when the kernel itself overruns the tick budget

`run_atc_operator_loop` fires `tracing::warn!("ATC operator tick exceeded
configured budget")` whenever the outer wall-clock iteration exceeds
`tick_budget_micros`. The configured budget, however, bounds the ATC
*kernel* compute time — not the outer loop, which also covers the periodic
5-minute durable rollup refresh, sweep queries, and population sync.

In production this produced ~343 WARNs per hour: every successful rollup
refresh tripped the threshold even though the kernel itself ran in
well under budget (~2.5ms vs 5ms budget on a 50ms refresh cycle).

Introduce a pure helper `atc_kernel_budget_exceeded(kernel_total_micros,
tick_duration_micros, tick_budget_micros)` and gate the WARN through it:

- With a live summary, the comparison is `kernel_total_micros > budget`,
  isolating the signal to real kernel slowdown.
- Without a live summary (which indicates a failed or skipped kernel
  run), we fall back to `tick_duration_micros > budget` so genuine
  starvation still surfaces rather than being silently dropped.
- Strict `>` is preserved so `kernel == budget` does not fire.

The WARN body is also enriched with `kernel_micros` and
`outer_loop_overhead_micros` so on-call can distinguish the two cases at
a glance without re-deriving the numbers by hand.

Four unit tests pin the behaviour:

- `atc_kernel_budget_warn_skips_outer_loop_overhead` — kernel within
  budget, outer loop over budget: no WARN (the exact regression).
- `atc_kernel_budget_warn_fires_on_kernel_overrun` — kernel over budget:
  WARN fires.
- `atc_kernel_budget_warn_falls_back_to_wall_clock_when_summary_missing`
  — no summary -> wall-clock fallback preserves existing safety net.
- `atc_kernel_budget_warn_kernel_at_budget_does_not_fire` — strict `>`
  semantics.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
